← all tutorials

Physical Clock Synchronization & NTP — interactive 📖 CLOCK_SYNC_NTP.md 📄 clock_sync_ntp.py

📖 Full guide (.md, green) — the long-form written explanation.  📄 Source code (.py, orange) — the ground-truth logic this page is verified against.
Quartz clocks drift at ~1 ppm, so two machines can disagree by ~1 s after 12 days. We can't make them perfect — we measure how wrong they are and correct them. Drag the network delays below and watch Cristian's offset, the Berkeley average, the NTP stratum tree, the drift bound, and Marzullo's outlier filter all recompute live. The tiny worked scenarios use the identical numbers as clock_sync_ntp.py.

① Cristian's algorithm — drag the delays, watch the offset & accuracy bound

What to do: the client sends at T0, the server stamps its time T1, the client receives at T2. Offset = T1 − (T0+T2)/2, accuracy = RTT/2. Drag d_out/d_in — when they're equal the estimate is exact; when asymmetric it's biased by (d_in−d_out)/2 but always within RTT/2.
Compare with clock_sync_ntp.py Section A. The GOLD scenario (server_ahead=12, d_out=3, d_in=5) must give theta=11.0, accuracy=4.0, true_offset=12, error=1.0.

② Berkeley algorithm — master averages all clocks, tells each slave how to nudge

What to do: edit each machine's clock (seconds relative to the master). The master takes the mean of all four and sends each one an adjustment = average − clock. Tick "poison with a liar" to see one bad clock drag the whole group.
Everyone converges to the average — they AGREE, even though none was "right". One liar drags the mean: that's why NTP runs Marzullo (panel ④) before averaging.

③ NTP stratum tree — error accumulates with every hop

Stratum 0 = atomic clock / GPS (the truth). Stratum 1 is wired to it. Each lower layer syncs from the one above and ADDS its own RTT/2 error. Stratum 16 = unsynchronized.
stratum 0 (truth) stratum 1 stratum 2 stratum 3

④ Marzullo's intersection — keep the largest overlapping clique, reject the liar

What to do: drag server D (the outlier). Three servers agree on the offset within ±2; Marzullo keeps the biggest clique and discards the rest. The shaded band is the region of agreement.
A=10, B=11, C=12 (all ±2). Matches clock_sync_ntp.py Section C.
This is NTP's outlier filter: no single server is ever trusted, but a QUORUM of overlapping intervals is.

⑤ Drift calculator — how far apart can two synced clocks get?

Quartz drift rho ≈ 1e-6 (1 ppm). A single clock can be off by rho·t; two clocks diverge by up to 2·rho·t. Drag the elapsed time and watch the divergence bound grow.
After 1 day: two clocks can differ by 0.1728 s. After a year: ~63 s. That's why NTP re-polls every ~17 min.

⑥ Why wall clocks bite — leap seconds, NTP steps, VM migration, Google smear

What to do: drag the NTP step. The wall-clock duration of a 0.3 s task can go negative when the clock jumps backward. Google's smear avoids this by spreading a leap second over 24 h (~11.57 ppm).
task really takes 0.3 s, straddling the step
Takeaway: never use wall-clock for durations or ordering. Use a monotonic clock (CLOCK_MONOTONIC / performance.now()) for elapsed time; logical clocks / HLC for event ordering.
check: … JS Cristian recompute matches clock_sync_ntp.py Section A (theta = +11.0, accuracy = 4.0).
